content.full_text:
Fern Polrrrc,q.l PRACrrcEs CouurssroN
428 J Street r Suite 620 r Sacramento, CA 95814-2329
(916) 322-5660 r Fax (916) 322-0886
September 12,2006
Lori Stone
Supen isor Jeff Stone, 3'd District
County of Riverside
29995 Evans Road, Suite 103
Sun City, California 92586
RE: Your Request for Advice
Our FiIe No. ,4'-06-153
Dear Ms. Stone:
This letter is in response to your request for advice regarding the mass mailing
provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").I
QUESTTON
Under the mass mailing provisions of the Act, may you send out four items
related to a 5K walk/run known as the "Race for Humanity," which each contain a
reference to "3'd District Supervisor Jeff Stone? "
CONCLUSION
You may send out the four items you have provided for our review. Providing
that you send each item separately, the mailings will each contain only a single reference
to Supervisor Stone and will not contain a photograph or signature. Accordingly, the
mailings fall within the exception for announcements to agency sponsored events,
provided by regulation 18901(b)(9), and are not prohibited mass mailings.2
' Gorr".oment Code sections 8 1000 - gl0l4. Commission regulations appeff at Title 2, sections
18109-18997, of the Califomia Code of Regulations.
2 We limit our analysis to the four items you have provided, any additional references to
Supervisor Stone contained within materials mailed with these four items, such as envelopes, cover letters,
or any other inserts, must also be analyzed. Should you wish to include any other references to Supervisor
Stone, other than those references contained in the four items you have already provided, and need
additional assistance to determine if the references are permitted under the mass mailing provisions of the
Act, it is advisable that you seek further assistance providing all relevant facts.


File No. A-06-153
Page No. 2
FACTS
The previous advice letter issued to you, Stone Advice Letter,No. 4-06-1 27, set
forth the following background facts. You are a Legislative Assistant on Women,s Issues
to 3'd District Supervisor Jeff Stone. You are "ro"r1ly organizingsupervisor Stone,s
Race for Humanity, which is a sanctioned 5K walVrunin conjunction with a l-mile
family fitness walk planned for Apt',l28,2007. In a telephone conversation on July 5,
2006, you indicated that the walklrun would bepartially funded by the County of
Riverside as the county's board of supervisors has approved the use of community
improvement designation funds to help fund the walk/run.
You have previously provided a copy of a "sponsorship Brochure" for review. In
the previous advice letter issued to you, Stone Advice Letter, supre, we concluded that
the mass mailing provisions of the Act did not limit the delivery of the Sponsorship
Brochure, as less than200 substantially similar items would be delivered in a singie
calendar month.
You have provided four additional items, which you have stated will each be
mailed out separately, at least a month after the Sponsorship Brochure. The four
additional items you have provided for our review include the following:
(1) Enty Form: 100,000 Entry Forms will be printed and distributed. The Entry
Forms will be distributed through the post office, as inserts in newspapers, and
byhand delivery.
(2) Team Tailgate Form: You foresee printing and distributing 199 or fewer Team
Tallgate Forms.
(3) Vendor/Kid's Expo Booth Reservation Form: You foresee printing and
distributing 199 or fewer Vendor/I(d's Expo Booth Reservation Forms.
(4) Volunteer Application: You estimate that atleast 500 Volunteer Applications
will be printed and distributed.
All four forms will also be available on your website for the public to download.
ANALYSIS
Section 89001 states that "no newsletter or other mass mailing shall be sent at
public expense." Regulation 18901(a) states that "except as provideJ in subdivision (b),
a mailing is prohibited by section 89001 if the following criteria are met:


File No. A-06-153
Page No. 3
"(1) Any item sent is delivered,by any means, to the recipient at
his or her residence, pTace of employment or business, or post office
box...
(2) The item sent either:
(A) Features an elected officer affiliated with the agencywhich
produces or sends the mailing, or
(B) Includes the name, office, photograph, or other reference to an
elected officer affiliated with the agency which produces or sends the
mailing, and is prepared or sent in cooperation, consultation, coordination,
or concert with the elected officer.
(3XA) Any of the costs of distribution is paid for with public
moneys; or
(B) costs of design, production, and printing exceeding $50.00 are
paid with public moneys, and the design, production, orprinting is done
with the intent of sending the item other than as permitted by this
regulation.
(4) More than two hundred substantially similar items are sent, in a
single calendar month, excluding any item sent in response to an
unsolicited request and any item described in subdivision (b).,,
In order for an item to be a prohibited "mass mailing" as defined under the Act,
all four, enumerated criteria listed above must be met. (See Reg. 18901, subds.(a)(l) -
(d(a).) If all four criteria under subdivision (a) apply, the mass mailing shall be
prohibited, barring the application of any exceptions. (see e.g., Reg. tggot, subds.(bxl)
- (bxl l) [listing the general exceptions to the Act's mass mailing piohibition].) 3 \ /\ /
Delivery of a o6Tangiblerr ltem
Subdivision (a)(1), of regulation 18901, only restricts items that are mailed or
delivered, by any means, to a person's residence, place of employment or business, or
post office box. If items are set out for the public to pick rrp o1tLeir own, or are handed
out in a public area,the restrictions of the regulation do noiapply. In addition, regulation
18901(a)(1), only restricts mass mailing items that are "tangiblei'such as a,,videJtape,
record, or button, or a written document." (See JohnstonAdvice Letter, No. a-OS-OiS.;
'Please note that there are other bodies 9f law, separate and apart from the Act,s mass mailing
provisions, thatmay apply to your situation including, but not limited to section B3l4 andpenal Code
section 424, whichproscribe the use ofpublic moneys for campaign-related activities by state and local
governmental entities. (See also stanson v. Mott (1976) 17 cat.3d206.)


File No. 4-06-153
Page No. 4
From the facts you have provided, some items may be "hand delivered.,, So long
as the items are not 'lnar,d delivered to a person's residence , place of employment or
business, or post office box, the items deiivered will not fall under the mass mailing
provisions of the Act. Additionally, you have indicated that the items will be posted
online. The Act itself does not limit the posting of the items on the Internet, as the mass
mailing provisions apply only to tangible items. (See CookAdvice Letter,No. I-02-123.)
However, you have indicated that alarge number of items will be sent and it
appears that most will be sent to a pe-rson_'s rerid"r"", place of employment or business,
or post office box. Except for items hand delivered ini public venue and the posting of
the items on the Interngt, delivering the four items through the postal service or with
newspaper inserts would constitute the delivery of atangible item under the mass mailing
provisions of the Act.
Featuring or Referencing an Elected Officers
Regulation 18901(a)Q), generally describes two categories of mass mailings that
ne regalated under the Act: (A) items that feature an elected officer affiliated with the
agency producing or sending the mailing, and (B) items that do not necessarily feature,
but include, a reference to an elected offi"". afiliated with the agency and are prepared
or sent in cooperation with that elected officer. A mass rnailingihat does not include any
reference to an elected officer affiliated with the agencythat priduces or distributes the
mailing is not subject to the restrictions of the regrilation.
It is clear tha] lhe four items you have provided feature an elected officer. Under
regulation 18901(c)(2), a mass mailing "[{Jeatures an elected officer,, if it includes the
elected officer's photograph or signature, or singles out the elected om"", Uy-the manner
of display of his or her name or office in the layout of the document, such as by
headlines, captions, type size, typeface, or type color. All four items you have provided
single out an elected officer by prominentrydispraylng .,3.d Dirt"; iil"rvi.o, r"rr
Stone" within the Race for Humanity logo.
Public Money
Pursuant to^subdivision (a)(3), the "public money" criterion of the regulation is
met if: "(A) Any of the costs of distribution are paid for wittr public *or"yrio. tlll (B)
costs of design, pro_duction, and printing exceed-ing $ 50.00 are paid *itr, i"uil" moneys,
and the design, production, or printing is done with the intent of sending the item other
than as permitted by this regulation.,,
In a telephone conversation on July5, 2006, you indicated that designated county
funds would be used to pay at least ro*" of th. a"rigrrirg, producing, printi"ng, and
distribution costs of the four items you have providJ f";;; reviewl- bir"" pliuu" funds


File No. 4-06-153
Page No. 5
will be used to distribute the items, the'public money" criterion of regulation
18901(a)(3) is met.
Over 200 Mailings Distributed
Regulation 18901 applies only to a "mass mailing" as defined in the Act. ..Mass
mailing" means over 200 substantially similarpieces ofmail. (Section g2041.5;
regulation 18901($@).) Regulation 18901(a)(4) cla1rfies that if fewer than20t of the
same or substantially similar items are sent in a calendar month, the regulation would not
restrict the mailing. Separate mailings maybe counted together if the i]ems sent are
substantially similar. Items are "substantially simil ar" if iny of the following applies:
"1. The items are identical, except for changes necessary to
identifiz the recipient and his or her address.
2- The items are intended to honor, commend, congratulate, or
recognize an individual or group, or individuals or grorrpr, fo, the same
event or occasion; are intended to celebrate or recognizi the same holiday;
or are intended to congratulate an individual or group, or individuals or
groups, on the same tlpe of event, such as birthdays or anniversaries.
3. Both of the following apply to the items mailed:
a. Most of the bills, legisl ation, governmental action, activities,
events, or issues of public concern mentioned in one item are mentioned in
the other.
b. Most of the information contained in one item is contained in
the other." (Regulation 1 890 I (c)(3XA).)
Thus, if mailings are substantially similar, they will be counted together to
determine whether more than 200 items are sent in a calendar month.
Based upon the facts submitted you have indicated that you will be delivering
100,000 Entry Forms, 199 or fewer Team Tailgate Forms, 199 or fewer Vendor/Kid,s
Expo Booth Reservation Forms, and an estimated 500 volunteer applications. Reviewing
the items, all four items identify the same activity or event, the Raci for Humanity.
Additionally, the four items all contain the same basic information such as the name of
the event, the location of the event, and the date of the event. Accordingly, most of the
information contained in any one item is also contained in the other items. We conclude
that the four items are substantially simil ar andwould be considered a mass mailing so
long as a total of 201 or more of the items are sent in a calendar month.


File No. A-06-153
Page No. 6
It should be noted that copies picked up at your offices or passed out at meetings
do not count toward the 2}}-itemlimit. (Reg. 18901(c)(a)(A); Miore Advice Letter, No.
A-93-307; Waggoner Advice Letter, No. 4-92-247; Alquist Advice Letter, No. I-g9-109.)
In addition, "[a] person who subscribes to newspapers or other periodicalspublished by
persons other than elected officers shall be deemed to have *ud" unsolicited requests for
materials published in those subscription publications.,, (Reg. l g90l (c)(a)@); see
Jenktns Advice Letter, No. 4-01-213.) Accordingly,items sent out as an insert in a
subscription newspaper do not count toward the 200-item limit so long as persons other
than elected officers publish the newspaper.
Exceptions
Based upon the facts you have provided it appears that well over 200 total items
will be sent to recipients at their home, place of employment or business, or post office
box; that the items will feature an elected official; and some cost of the distribution will
be paid for with public moneys. Accordingly, the mass mailing provisions will prohibit
the mailing of the four items unless an exception of the provisions applies.
Regulation 18901(b) provides avariety of exceptions to the four-criteria mass
mailing prohibition. In pertinentpart, subdivision (b)(9X2) provides an exception for an
"announcement of any official agency event or events for which the agency is providing
the use of its facilities or staff or other financial support." However, this exception onlf
applies if the announcement does "not include the elected officer's photograph or
signafure" and includes "only one mention of the elected officer's ru-",,L.ept as
permitted elsewhere in regulation 18901.
In previous advice letters addressing regulation 13901(bX9), we have provided
that mailings can "make one mention" of an elected officer where iseor6;10)1a1r; o.
(2) applies. (See Godwin Advice Letter, No. 4-04-004; Mathys Advice Liti"r,No. I-9g-
087; and Kamp Advice Letter, No. A-97-623(a).) The four items you have submitted for
our review each have a single reference to the elected officer's name, ,.3.d District
Supervisor Jeff Stone." The Entry Form also contains several personal pronouns
included within the following phrases: (l) "help us reach our goal,,, (2)i,*"reserve the
idht," and (3) "our inagural [sic] partners." Additionally, the volunteer application
contains a personal pronoun and the use of the word "office" in the phrase i.call our
office."
Consequently, we must now determine whether your use of personal pronouns
and the use of the word "office" are impermissible references to the elected officer.
Reviewing regulation 18901(b)(9), mass mailing fall within the exception for
Elnnouncements of agency sponsored events so long as the announcement does ..not
include the elected officer's photograph or signature" and includes "only a single mention
of the elected officer's name."


File No. 4-06-153
Page No. 7
Prior advice letters have concluded that personal pronouns such as ,,I,, afld,,we,,
were references to the elected officer "affiliateiwith the agency which produces or sends
the mailing," afld that the use of the word "office" was also a personal reference to the
elected officer. (See Perea Advice Letter,No. 4-02-260.) However, under the facts of
these prior advice letters, the personal pronouns used and the references to an elected
officer's office were clearly related,to a specific elected officer.
In the Fisicaro Advice Letter, No. 4-96-230, we concluded that references to the
t-"*f "house captains" and "volunteer coordinators" alone, without other references to
the elected officers whom had acted as house captains and volunteer coordinators, were
not references to the elected officers. Your use ofpersonal pronouns and the lerm
"offi9.t" are cotnparable to the use of the terms 'house captains,, and,.volunteer
coordinators," as analyzed in the Fisicaro Advice Letter,-supra,as it is not apparent
without additional references thatthepersonal pronouns or the term ..ofi[ice,, refer to
Supervisor Stone.
Based on the facts submitted, we conclude that your use of personal pronouns and
the word o'office" are not references to Supervisor Ston!. Therefori, *" .orr'"lrde that the
four items you have provided for our review fit within the exception for announcernents
to agency sponsored events, provided by regulation 1 8901(b)(9), as the items Jo .,ot
contain a photograph or signature of Supervisor Stone and oniyinclude one reference to
>upervlsor Stone.'
If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (gt6) 322-5660.
Sincerely,
Luisa Menchaca
General Counsel
By:
G. Lau
Counsel, Legal Division
BGL:rd
I:\Adviceltrs\06-153
a we caution that this conclusion is sensitive to the fact that allitems will be mailed separately.
Should you chose to mail more than one of the items you have provided in a single envelope, the mailing
would include multiple references to Supervisor Stoni's name and would not fal'i within thf exception of
18e01(bxexA)(1).

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: Under the mass mailing provisions of the Act, may you send out four items
related to a 5K walk/run known as the "Race for Humanity," which each contain a
reference to "3'd District Supervisor Jeff Stone? "

CONCLUSION: You may send out the four items you have provided for our review. Providing
that you send each item separately, the mailings will each contain only a single reference
to Supervisor Stone and will not contain a photograph or signature. Accordingly, the
mailings fall within the exception for announcements to agency sponsored events,
provided by regulation 18901(b)(9), and are not prohibited mass mailings.2
' Gorr".oment Code sections 8 1000 - gl0l4. Commission regulations appeff at Title 2, sections
18109-18997, of the Califomia Code of Regulations.
2 We limit our analysis to the four items you have provided, any additional references to
Supervisor Stone contained within materials mailed with these four items, such as envelopes, cover letters,
or any other inserts, must also be analyzed. Should you wish to include any other references to Supervisor
Stone, other than those references contained in the four items you have already provided, and need
additional assistance to determine if the references are permitted under the mass mailing provisions of the
Act, it is advisable that you seek further assistance providing all relevant facts.
